project: Ikeru - Japanese Learning iOS + Apple Watch App

sotaku_analysis.description: Sottaku (also spelled Sotaku) is a multi-language learning platform supporting Chinese (Simplified & Traditional), Japanese, Korean, and English. It uses a freemium model where all core materials -- grammar guides, quizzes, and flashcards -- are permanently free. Available on iOS, Android, and web. 4.9-star Google Play rating with 1.6M+ downloads.
sotaku_analysis.methodology: Built on the FSRS (Free Spaced Repetition Scheduler) algorithm, the same ML-based scheduler used by Anki. FSRS uses machine learning trained on 700M+ reviews to predict optimal review timing, personalized to each learner. Covers vocabulary flashcards, grammar guides for all proficiency levels, interactive quizzes, and kanji/kana/hangul character instruction.
key_features: FSRS-based spaced repetition (same algorithm as Anki)
Free grammar guides, quizzes, and flashcards
Browser extension (Sottaku-Yomitan) to add words from web browsing to flashcards
strengths: Generous free tier -- all core learning materials free forever
Uses scientifically-backed FSRS algorithm for optimal retention
Browser extension for contextual vocabulary mining from real content
Multi-language support makes it appealing to polyglots
weaknesses: Relatively new app -- smaller content library compared to established tools like WaniKani
No dedicated speaking or pronunciation practice features
Grammar explanations may be less comprehensive than Bunpro or textbooks
Limited offline capabilities compared to native apps
elements_to_adopt: FSRS algorithm as the core SRS engine -- proven 20-30% fewer reviews for same retention
Browser/content extension for vocabulary mining from real-world content
Freemium model with generous free tier builds user base
Leaderboard and social features for motivation
Clean progress tracking with achievement systems
Multi-skill integration in a single platform

rtk_assessment.qualified_recommendation: RTK remains recommended as the fastest method for learning kanji meanings (recognition), but only when paired with supplementary resources for readings, vocabulary, and context. It is increasingly seen as one option among several rather than the definitive approach.
rtk_assessment.methodology: Breaks each of 2,200 kanji into component parts (radicals/primitives) and assigns mnemonic stories linking components to the kanji's English keyword meaning. Teaches kanji in a logical order where simpler components are learned before complex characters that contain them. Does NOT teach Japanese readings (on'yomi/kun'yomi) or vocabulary in Volume 1.
volumes: Volume 1: 2,200 kanji meanings and writing (the most popular volume) - $34
Volume 2: Systematic introduction of on'yomi and kun'yomi readings for kanji from Vol 1
Volume 3: 800 additional kanji beyond the standard joyo set
pros: Fastest method to recognize meanings of 2,200 kanji
Systematic component-based decomposition makes complex kanji manageable
Mnemonic stories create strong memory hooks
Logical learning order (simple -> complex) builds on prior knowledge
Well-established community with extensive Anki decks available
Covers all joyo kanji in a single structured course
cons: Volume 1 does NOT teach readings -- a critical gap for actual Japanese literacy
Teaches kanji in isolation from vocabulary and real usage context
English keywords can be misleading about actual Japanese usage
Requires 3-6 months of dedicated study before you can read any Japanese
Must be paired with SRS (Anki) for effective retention -- book alone insufficient
Some mnemonics are weak or culturally insensitive
No audio component -- purely visual/text-based learning
Controversial ordering -- not frequency-based, so common kanji may come late

alternatives.name: WaniKani
alternatives.approach: Web-based SRS teaching kanji meanings, readings, AND vocabulary together with built-in mnemonics
alternatives.advantage_over_rtk: Integrated readings and vocabulary; built-in SRS; active community

alternatives.name: Kodansha Kanji Learner's Course (KKLC)
alternatives.approach: Textbook teaching 2,300 kanji with readings, vocabulary in context, and stroke order
alternatives.advantage_over_rtk: Teaches readings and vocabulary alongside meanings; frequency-ordered; sample vocab uses only previously learned kanji

alternatives.name: Kanji Garden
alternatives.approach: App-based component learning with SRS, animations, readings, and example words
alternatives.advantage_over_rtk: Interactive with stroke animations; teaches readings; built-in SRS; mobile-friendly

alternatives.name: Kanji Damage
alternatives.approach: Free web resource teaching 1,700 kanji with humor-based mnemonics including readings
alternatives.advantage_over_rtk: Free; includes readings; humorous mnemonics for memorability

rtk_assessment.recommendation_for_ikeru: Adopt the component-based decomposition approach from RTK but integrate readings and vocabulary from the start (like KKLC/WaniKani). Use FSRS for scheduling. This is the biggest opportunity gap -- no app combines RTK's systematic decomposition with immediate reading integration and modern ML-based SRS.

reading: Component-based kanji decomposition (radicals -> kanji -> vocabulary -> sentences) with spaced repetition
Graded readers starting from N5 level, progressing through JLPT levels
Extensive reading with dictionary lookup tools (Yomitan/browser extensions)
i+1 comprehensible input -- reading material slightly above current level
Sentence mining from native content (manga, news, novels) with SRS review
Context-based vocabulary learning (words learned in sentences, not isolation)
KKLC-style approach: only use kanji the learner has already studied in examples
writing: Stroke order animation and guided practice with immediate feedback
Radical/component recognition before full kanji writing
Spaced repetition for writing practice (recall kanji from meaning/reading)
Production-focused exercises: compose sentences using learned grammar/vocabulary
Handwriting recognition technology for stroke accuracy verification
Progressive complexity: trace -> guided writing -> free writing -> composition
Writing kanji by hand shown to improve recognition and long-term retention
listening: Shadowing technique: listen and repeat simultaneously, matching rhythm/intonation/pronunciation
Comprehensible input at i+1 level with graduated difficulty
Podcast-based learning with transcripts (e.g., Comprehensible Japanese, Nihongo con Teppei)
Active listening exercises with comprehension questions
Speed-graded content: slow -> natural speed progression
Pitch accent awareness training (critical for natural comprehension)
Dictation exercises: write what you hear
Media immersion with graded subtitles (Japanese subs -> no subs progression)
speaking: Shadowing for pronunciation and rhythm development
AI-powered conversation practice with real-time feedback (Kaiwa, Talkpal, SpeakPal)
Roleplay scenarios for real-world situations (restaurant, directions, self-introduction)
Pitch accent training with visual feedback
Record and compare: record yourself, compare to native audio
Speech recognition for pronunciation accuracy assessment
Start speaking early -- even simple phrases build confidence and muscle memory
general: Spaced Repetition System (FSRS algorithm preferred over SM-2): 20-30% more efficient than traditional SRS
i+1 Comprehensible Input (Krashen): content slightly above current level
Active recall over passive recognition: typing/producing answers > multiple choice
Interleaving: mix different skills in study sessions rather than blocking
Consistent daily practice (30-60 min/day) beats intensive weekend cramming
Multi-modal learning: combine visual, auditory, and kinesthetic (writing) for stronger memory
Immersion-based learning with real native content as soon as possible
Test-enhanced learning: frequent low-stakes quizzing improves retention
Metacognitive training: teach learners to assess their own knowledge gaps
Goal-oriented progression tied to JLPT levels for measurable milestones
srs_best_practices: FSRS algorithm: ML-based, personalized intervals, 20-30% fewer reviews than SM-2
New cards per day: 10-20 for sustainable long-term progress
Review consistency: better to review daily than skip days and pile up
Card design: one concept per card, clear and unambiguous
Context-rich cards: include example sentences, audio, and images
Leech detection: flag cards that are repeatedly failed for redesign
Mature card intervals: up to 1 year between reviews for well-known items

N5_beginner.description: Basic greetings, self-introduction, simple daily conversations

N4_elementary.description: Basic daily conversation, read simple texts

N3_intermediate.description: Everyday situations, read newspaper headlines, bridge level

N2_upper_intermediate.description: Read newspapers/articles, understand most conversations, business Japanese

N1_advanced.description: Read complex texts, understand nuanced speech, professional proficiency

competitive_landscape.name: Anki
competitive_landscape.approach: Open-source flashcard app with FSRS algorithm. Fully customizable decks, community-shared content. The gold standard for SRS.
competitive_landscape.strengths: Maximum flexibility, FSRS algorithm, massive community decks, completely free on desktop, works offline
competitive_landscape.gaps: Steep learning curve, intimidating settings, no built-in content, ugly default UI, no structured learning path, requires significant setup discipline, no speaking/listening features

competitive_landscape.name: WaniKani
competitive_landscape.approach: Web-based kanji+vocabulary learning with built-in mnemonics and SRS. 60 levels covering 2,074 kanji and 6,528 vocabulary words.
competitive_landscape.strengths: Excellent mnemonics for both meanings AND readings, built-in SRS, active community with user scripts, structured progression, no setup required
competitive_landscape.gaps: No grammar, no speaking, no listening, no writing practice, rigid pacing (early levels feel slow), kanji-only focus, expensive for what it offers, web-only (no native mobile app), locked progression frustrates advanced learners

competitive_landscape.name: Bunpro
competitive_landscape.approach: Grammar-focused SRS covering 900+ grammar points organized by JLPT level. Active recall through typed answers.
competitive_landscape.strengths: Best grammar SRS available, forces production (typing), organized by JLPT level, links to external resources, textbook path alignment (Genki, Tobira, etc.)
competitive_landscape.gaps: Grammar only -- no kanji, vocabulary, speaking, or listening, strict answer acceptance frustrates users, brief explanations need supplementing, isolated grammar points lack real-world context

competitive_landscape.name: Duolingo Japanese
competitive_landscape.approach: Gamified course with bite-sized lessons covering vocabulary, grammar, kana, and basic kanji through translation exercises.
competitive_landscape.strengths: Extremely polished UI/UX, strong gamification (streaks, leagues), massive user base, free tier is generous, good for absolute beginners
competitive_landscape.gaps: Shallow grammar explanations, recognition over production, brutally slow progression, limited kanji coverage, no pitch accent, grammar points infrequent and poorly explained, polite vs casual forms glossed over, insufficient for anything beyond N5

competitive_landscape.name: LingoDeer
competitive_landscape.approach: Structured Japanese course with detailed grammar explanations, vocabulary, reading, and listening exercises up to ~JLPT N3.
competitive_landscape.strengths: Much better grammar explanations than Duolingo, natural example sentences, good audio quality, teaches all three writing systems, comprehensive curriculum for beginners
competitive_landscape.gaps: Content stops around N4-N3 level, no speaking practice, no handwriting practice, limited advanced content, some bugs in audio, expensive for limited content depth

competitive_landscape.name: Migaku
competitive_landscape.approach: Immersion-based learning platform with browser extension for subtitle mining, reading tools, and AI-assisted study from native content (Netflix, YouTube, manga).
competitive_landscape.strengths: Best for immersion learners, AI-generated subtitles, sentence mining from real content, media-based learning, comprehensive courses for beginners, fastest path to advanced fluency
competitive_landscape.gaps: Complex setup (browser extension + desktop app), confusing for beginners, requires existing content subscriptions, not a standalone app, better for intermediate+ learners, no Apple Watch support

competitive_landscape.name: Kanji Garden
competitive_landscape.approach: Component-based kanji learning with SRS, stroke animations, readings, example words, and audio. Personalized study plans.
competitive_landscape.strengths: Component-based learning order, stroke order animations, built-in SRS with 1-year mature intervals, readings and vocabulary included, clean mobile app, good for ADHD learners
competitive_landscape.gaps: Kanji-focused only, no grammar, no speaking/listening, relatively small user community, limited advanced features

competitive_landscape.name: Jalup / Nihongo Lessons
competitive_landscape.approach: i+1 sentence-based learning through flashcards. Japanese-English for beginners, then Japanese-Japanese. Full native audio on all cards.
competitive_landscape.strengths: Excellent i+1 methodology, contextual learning, transitions to Japanese-only explanations, native audio throughout, includes manga-based content
competitive_landscape.gaps: Jalup itself shut down (content now in Nihongo app), no speaking practice, no handwriting, limited interactivity, deck-based format may feel monotonous

competitive_landscape.name: Takoboto
competitive_landscape.approach: Japanese-English dictionary with 200,000+ entries, conjugations, example sentences, and built-in flashcard functionality.
competitive_landscape.strengths: Comprehensive dictionary (200K+ words), conjugation lookup, example sentences, flashcard export to AnkiDroid, 4.66 star rating, offline capable
competitive_landscape.gaps: Dictionary-first tool, not a structured learning app, no SRS scheduling, no grammar instruction, no speaking/listening, basic flashcard functionality

competitive_landscape.name: Kaiwa
competitive_landscape.approach: AI-powered Japanese conversation practice with real-time feedback on grammar, pronunciation, and natural speech patterns.
competitive_landscape.strengths: Best AI speaking practice for Japanese, real-time feedback, no scheduling needed, judgment-free practice, scenario-based conversations
competitive_landscape.gaps: Speaking only -- no reading, writing, or kanji learning, requires internet, AI sometimes makes errors, limited vocabulary instruction

competitive_landscape.name: Talkpal
competitive_landscape.approach: AI conversation tutor with real-time transcription, grammar correction, and persona modes (character-based scenarios).
competitive_landscape.strengths: Real-time grammar highlighting, persona mode for varied contexts (formal/casual), character-based scenarios, strong market momentum in 2025
competitive_landscape.gaps: Speaking-focused only, no kanji/writing features, subscription required for full access, newer app with less proven track record

apple_watch_japanese_apps.name: TokeiTango
apple_watch_japanese_apps.description: Flashcard app exclusively for Apple Watch with 9,000+ words across JLPT N5-N1. Updated for watchOS 26 with Liquid Glass design.
apple_watch_japanese_apps.limitations: Vocabulary flashcards only, no grammar or kanji learning, limited interaction model

apple_watch_japanese_apps.name: LingoTime
apple_watch_japanese_apps.description: Japanese vocabulary and kanji learning across all Apple platforms (watchOS, iOS, macOS). Features widgets, iCloud sync, and Smart SRS.

apple_watch_japanese_apps.name: Wakaru
apple_watch_japanese_apps.description: Augmented language learning assistant for Apple Watch with voice-based translation, context understanding, and roleplay.
apple_watch_japanese_apps.limitations: Translation/assistant focus rather than structured learning

apple_watch_japanese_apps.name: Daily Kanji
apple_watch_japanese_apps.description: Daily kanji notifications by JLPT level for passive learning on Apple Watch.
apple_watch_japanese_apps.limitations: Notification-based only, no active learning or SRS

apple_watch_japanese_apps.name: Hiragana Having It Up My Watch
apple_watch_japanese_apps.description: Hiragana learning and writing practice specifically for Apple Watch.

ios_watchos_landscape.market_gap_analysis: No existing Apple Watch app provides comprehensive Japanese learning. Current Watch apps are limited to simple flashcards, notifications, or narrow scope (hiragana only). There is NO Watch app that combines: SRS-based review, kanji/vocabulary/grammar, listening snippets, or progress tracking synced with a full iOS app. This represents a significant greenfield opportunity.
ios_japanese_app_ecosystem: Kanji Teacher: stroke order analysis with position/direction/order checking for all 2,136 joyo kanji
Kanji Koi: JLPT N5-N1 coverage with drawing and swipe review modes, stroke guidance
KanjiQ: 13,000 kanji with 6,000 stroke order animations, trace-and-compare
Kanji Study: stroke detection with instant feedback, SRS reviews
iKanji touch 2: writing practice with stroke animation and stroke count display

ai_integration_trends: LLM-powered conversation tutors are the fastest-growing segment -- Kaiwa, Talkpal, SpeakPal, Langua all launched/scaled 2024-2025
Google's LearnLM (integrated into Gemini 2.5) uses Socratic questioning and adaptive instruction, preferred over GPT-4o by 31% in learning scenarios
AI-generated subtitles and real-time translation are becoming standard (Migaku leads here)
Persona/roleplay modes in AI tutors (e.g., Talkpal's character scenarios) improve engagement and contextual learning
Students using AI speaking practice showed 75% improvement in speaking scores over 8 weeks (2025 study)
LearnLM students were 5.5 percentage points more likely to solve novel problems vs human tutors alone
Multimodal AI (vision + audio + text) enables new features: OCR for handwriting feedback, image-based vocabulary, audio pronunciation scoring
FSRS algorithm (ML-based SRS) reached mainstream adoption in 2025 -- now in Anki, RemNote, Mochi Cards, and multiple learning platforms
RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) enables accurate grammar explanations and example generation without hallucination
On-device ML (Core ML, Apple FoundationModels) enables offline SRS optimization and handwriting recognition

gemini_2_5_pro.best_for: Complex grammar explanations, nuanced conversation simulation, essay correction

gemini_2_5_flash.best_for: Real-time conversation practice, quick vocabulary queries, sentence generation

gemini_2_5_flash_lite.best_for: Simple lookups, card generation, basic translation verification

gemini_free_tier_assessment.practical_limits_for_ikeru: With Flash-Lite at 1,000 RPD, a user doing 30-60 min/day of study could realistically make 50-100 API calls per session. Flash at 500 RPD is sufficient for conversation practice sessions. Pro at 100 RPD should be reserved for complex tasks (essay correction, grammar explanation generation). Combined, the free tier supports a viable personal-use app.
important_caveats: December 2025 saw 50-80% quota reductions -- limits may change again
Limits are per-project, not per-API-key -- multiple keys don't help
Actual limits may vary from documented limits (check AI Studio dashboard)
Free tier data may be used for model improvement (privacy consideration)
Rate limits are not guaranteed and can be further restricted
For a personal app this is fine; for distribution, paid tier may be needed
alternative_free_options: Groq (free tier with Llama/Mixtral models -- very fast inference)
OpenRouter (aggregates free tiers from multiple providers)
On-device Apple FoundationModels (iOS 26+, no API cost, offline capable)
Ollama for local inference during development

market_context: Japanese learning platform market: $860M in 2025, projected $2.5B by 2032 (16.4% CAGR)
Global language learning market exceeds $25B with mobile apps as fastest-growing segment
65-70% of language learners now prefer app-based learning over classrooms
Japanese language learners increased 17% globally since 2023, driven by anime/manga/J-pop cultural interest
Asia-Pacific commands 45.75% of online language learning market revenue
FSI estimates 2,200 hours for English speakers to achieve Japanese professional proficiency -- highest difficulty category
Japanese is consistently in top 10 most-studied languages globally
Apple ecosystem users tend to have higher willingness to pay for quality apps
Indie/solo developer Japanese apps have successfully competed (Manabi Reader won 'best iOS Japanese learning tool 2025')
user_sentiment: Biggest frustration: needing 4-6 separate apps to cover all skills (kanji app + grammar app + speaking app + dictionary + reading tool)
Anki users love the power but hate the UI, setup complexity, and ugly default experience
WaniKani users frustrated by rigid pacing -- early levels feel too slow, can't skip known content
Duolingo users hit a wall around N4 level -- insufficient depth for real progress
Bunpro's strict typing requirements cause frustration when multiple valid answers exist
LingoDeer praised for grammar explanations but content stops too early (N4-N3)
Strong demand for AI conversation partners that understand formality levels and context
Users want progress tracking that shows concrete JLPT-level advancement
Handwriting practice is valued but poorly served -- most apps have limited stroke recognition
Apple Watch users want micro-learning sessions for commute/waiting time
Privacy-conscious users prefer on-device processing over cloud-dependent features
Community and social features (streaks, leaderboards) are effective motivation tools but not enough alone
Advanced learners (N2+) feel severely underserved by most apps
timing_and_opportunity: MASSIVE OPPORTUNITY: No single app covers all 4 skills (reading, writing, listening, speaking) with modern ML/AI -- the market is fragmented into single-skill tools
Apple Watch companion for Japanese learning is essentially greenfield -- existing apps are minimal flashcard viewers
FSRS algorithm is now proven and open-source, ready for integration without licensing issues
Gemini free tier provides viable LLM access for a personal app's conversation/tutoring features at zero cost
Apple's FoundationModels framework (iOS 26+) enables on-device LLM for offline features -- perfect for Watch
LearnLM research validates AI tutoring effectiveness -- replicable patterns for grammar explanation and Socratic questioning
The indie developer success stories (Manabi Reader, Kanji Garden) prove there's room for quality solo-dev apps
Growing interest in evidence-based, data-exportable learning tools among technical users
JLPT-aligned progression gives clear product milestones and marketing angles (N5 starter -> N1 complete)
Cultural moment: anime/manga popularity driving Japanese learning demand to historic highs
risks_and_considerations: Gemini free tier limits could be reduced further (already cut 50-80% in Dec 2025) -- need fallback strategy
Content creation is the biggest bottleneck: 10,000+ vocabulary items, 700+ grammar points, audio recordings, example sentences
Competing with well-funded apps (Duolingo: $7B valuation, WaniKani: established community) on production quality
Speech recognition accuracy for Japanese (pitch accent, particles) is still imperfect across all platforms
Apple Watch screen size severely limits interaction complexity -- must design for glanceable micro-learning
App Store discovery is extremely competitive -- need strong differentiation messaging
Maintaining content accuracy requires native speaker review (especially grammar explanations and example sentences)
JLPT content is not officially published -- community-sourced JLPT lists may have inaccuracies
User retention beyond N4 level drops significantly across all apps -- advanced content is harder to gamify
Data export feature is a differentiator but adds engineering complexity (CSV, JSON, Anki export formats)
Scope creep risk: covering all 4 skills is ambitious for a solo developer -- consider phased release (e.g., reading+writing first, then listening+speaking)
On-device ML models need careful optimization for Watch hardware constraints
Privacy regulations (GDPR if distributing in EU) apply to any user learning data collected

recommended_architecture_for_ikeru.core_srs_engine: FSRS algorithm (open-source, MIT licensed, Swift port available via awesome-fsrs GitHub repo)
recommended_architecture_for_ikeru.kanji_system: Component-based decomposition (RTK-inspired) WITH integrated readings and vocabulary (KKLC-inspired). Teach radicals -> kanji meaning+reading -> vocabulary in context.
recommended_architecture_for_ikeru.grammar_system: JLPT-organized grammar points with typed production exercises (Bunpro-inspired) enhanced by LLM-generated explanations and examples
recommended_architecture_for_ikeru.listening_system: Graded audio content with comprehension exercises, shadowing mode with recording/comparison, pitch accent visualization
recommended_architecture_for_ikeru.speaking_system: Gemini API-powered conversation practice with scenario-based roleplay, pronunciation feedback via iOS Speech framework
recommended_architecture_for_ikeru.writing_system: Stroke order animations with PencilKit/touch-based handwriting recognition and accuracy scoring
recommended_architecture_for_ikeru.apple_watch: Micro-review sessions (SRS flashcard reviews), daily kanji notification, listening comprehension snippets, progress dashboard complications
recommended_architecture_for_ikeru.data_layer: SwiftData for local persistence, CloudKit for sync, export to CSV/JSON/Anki formats
recommended_architecture_for_ikeru.ai_integration: Gemini Flash-Lite for routine tasks (1000 RPD), Flash for conversation (500 RPD), Pro for complex explanations (100 RPD). Apple FoundationModels for offline fallback.
recommended_architecture_for_ikeru.progress_tracking: JLPT-level dashboard, skill radar chart (reading/writing/listening/speaking), streak tracking, detailed statistics with data export
